content.problemStatement:
After solving some Number Theory related problems KBG challenged VD to solve following problem.

You are given an array AAA consisting of NNN positive integers and QQQ queries. Each query is of the form:


LLL RRR: Given two integers LLL and RRR (1≤L<R≤N)(1 \leq L < R \leq N)(1≤L<R≤N), let BBB denote the subarray A[L,R]A[L, R]A[L,R]. Find the **maximum** possible GCD of any subsequence of BBB which has length **greater** than 111.
For each query, find the **maximum** possible GCDGCDGCD of any subsequence in the given range having length **greater** than 111.


Input Format

First line contains TTT, number of test cases. Description of the test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a two space-separated integer NNN and QQQ - length of the array and number of queries respectively.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,...,ANA_1 , A_2 , ... , A_NA1​,A2​,...,AN​.
Next QQQ lines of each test case contains two space-separated integers LLL and RRR.


Output Format
For each test case, print QQQ lines. The ithi^{th}ith line contains one integer - the answer for the ithi^{th}ith query.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 150
Sum of Q over all test cases ≤ 150
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- The first line contains two integers N and Q.
- The second line contains N space‑separated positive integers A1 … AN.
- Each of the next Q lines contains two integers L and R (1 ≤ L < R ≤ N), describing a query.
All indices are 1‑based.
content.outputFormat: For each query, output a single line containing the maximum possible GCD of any subsequence of A[L..R] whose length is greater than 1. The answers for the queries of all test cases are printed in the order they appear.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For query (1,5) the pair (5,15) gives gcd 5, which is the maximum. For query (4,6) the pair (6,8) gives gcd 2, which is the maximum.

solution.approach:
For a subsequence of length at least two the GCD cannot be larger than the GCD of any pair inside the subsequence. Therefore the optimal answer for a range [L,R] is simply the maximum GCD among all unordered pairs (i,j) with L ≤ i < j ≤ R. Because N ≤ 15 in the test data, we can evaluate every pair directly for each query. For each query we iterate i from L‑1 to R‑1 and j from i+1 to R‑1, compute gcd(A[i],A[j]) using the Euclidean algorithm and keep the largest value.
The overall complexity per query is O((R‑L+1)^2) ≤ O(N^2) which is at most 225 operations for the given limits, easily fast enough.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * Q * N^2) – with the given limits this is at most a few thousand elementary operations.
